title: Multiply matrices
description: Find the result of a multiplication of two given matrices.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text F = \left[\begin{array}{rr}0 & 4 \\ 5 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\text D = \left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & 5 \\ 3 & 0\end{array}\right]$.

** Let $\text {H = FD}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{F}$ and the first column of $\text{D}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{0} & \maroonC{4} \\ 5 & 2\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{2} & 5 \\ \maroonC{3} &  0\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(0,4)\cdot(2,3)\\\\
&=0 \cdot 2 + 4\cdot 3\\\\
&=12
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}12 & 0 \\ 16 & 25\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 4 \\ 5 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\text C = \left[\begin{array}{rr}1 & 2 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right]$. 

** Let $\text {H = EC}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 2]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{C}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{-1} & \maroonC{4} \\ 5 & 2\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{1} & 2 \\ \maroonC{-1} &  1\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(-1,4)\cdot(1,-1)\\\\
&=-1 \cdot 1 + 4\cdot -1\\\\
&=-5
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

 $ \left[\begin{array}{rr}-5 & 2 \\ 3 & 12\end{array}\right] $

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text B = \left[\begin{array}{rr}5 & -2 \\ -1 & 5\end{array}\right]$ and $\text A = \left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2\end{array}\right]$. 

** Let $\text {H = BA}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{B}$ and the first column of $\text{A}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{5} & \maroonC{-2} \\ -1 & 5\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{2} & 0 \\ \maroonC{0} &  2\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(5,-2)\cdot(2,0)\\\\
&=5 \cdot 2 - 2\cdot 0\\\\
&=10
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}= \left[\begin{array}{rr}10 & -4 \\ -2 & 10\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text B = \left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & 3 \\ 1 & 3\end{array}\right]$  and $\text A = \left[\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 3 \\ -1 & 0\end{array}\right]$. 

** Let $\text {H = BA}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{B}$ and the first column of $\text{A}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{2} & \maroonC{3} \\ 1 & 3\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{-1} & 3 \\ \maroonC{-1} &  0\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(2,3)\cdot(-1,-1)\\\\
&=2 \cdot -1 + 3\cdot -1\\\\
&=-5
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}-5 & 6 \\ -4 & 3\end{array}\right] $

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text B = \left[\begin{array}{rr}1 & 4 \\ 1 & 3\end{array}\right]$ and $\text D = \left[\begin{array}{rr}4 & 3 \\ 0 & -1\end{array}\right]$. 

** Let $\text {H = BD}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{B}$ and the first column of $\text{D}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{1} & \maroonC{4} \\ 1 & 3\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{4} & 3 \\ \maroonC{0} &  -1\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(1,4)\cdot(4,0)\\\\
&=1 \cdot 4 + 4\cdot 0\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text{H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}4 & -1 \\ 4 & 0\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rr}-1 & -2 \\ -2 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and $\text A = \left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & 0 \\ 2 & -1\end{array}\right]$. 

** Let $\text {H = EA}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{A}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{-1} & \maroonC{-2} \\ -2 & -1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{2} & 0 \\ \maroonC{2} &  -1\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(-1,-2)\cdot(2,2)\\\\
&=-1 \cdot 2 - 2\cdot 2\\\\
&=-6
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}-6 & 2 \\ -6 & 1\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text D = \left[\begin{array}{rrr}-1 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right]$ and $\text F = \left[\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 1 \\ -1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4\end{array}\right]$



** Let $\text {H = DF}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{D}$ and the first column of $\text{F}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{-1} & \maroonC{0} & \maroonC{4} \\ 2 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{-1} & 1 \\ \maroonC{-1} &  3 \\ \maroonC{2} &  4\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(-1,0,4)\cdot(-1,-1,2)\\\\
&=-1 \cdot -1+0\cdot -1 + 4\cdot 2\\\\
&=9
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}9 & 15 \\ -2 & 2\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rrr}1 & 4 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\text B = \left[\begin{array}{rr}1 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -2\end{array}\right]$



** Let $\text {H = EB}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{B}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{1} & \maroonC{4} & \maroonC{2} \\ 1 & 2 & 2\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{1} & 3 \\ \maroonC{2} &  3 \\ \maroonC{2} &  -2\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(1,4,2)\cdot(1,2,2)\\\\
&=1 \cdot 1+4\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 2\\\\
&=13
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}13 & 11 \\ 9 & 5\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rrr}0 & 3 & 5 \\ 5 & 5 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\text D = \left[\begin{array}{rr}3 & 4 \\ 3 & -2 \\ 4 & -2\end{array}\right]$



** Let $\text {H = ED}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{D}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{0} & \maroonC{3} & \maroonC{5} \\ 5 & 5 & 2\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{3} & 4 \\ \maroonC{3} &  -2 \\ \maroonC{4} &  -2\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(0,3,5)\cdot(3,3,4)\\\\
&=0 \cdot 3+3\cdot 3 + 5\cdot 4\\\\
&=29
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}29 & -16 \\ 38 & 6\end{array}\right] $

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rrr}1 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 4\end{array}\right]$ and $\text A = \left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & 4 \\ 2 & -2 \\ -1 & 5\end{array}\right]$

** Let $\text {H = EA}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{A}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{1} & \maroonC{-1} & \maroonC{1} \\ 2 & 3 & 4\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{2} & 4 \\ \maroonC{2} &  -2 \\ \maroonC{-1} &  5\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(1,-1,1)\cdot(2,2,-1)\\\\
&=1 \cdot 2-1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot -1\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 11 \\ 6 & 22\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rrr}1 & -1 & 5 \\ 5 & 5 & 0\end{array}\right]$ and  $\text F = \left[\begin{array}{rr}-2 & -1 \\ 5 & 2 \\ 5 & -2\end{array}\right]$

** Let $\text {H = EF}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{F}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{1} & \maroonC{-1} & \maroonC{5} \\ 5 & 5 & 0\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{-2} & -1 \\ \maroonC{5} &  2 \\ \maroonC{5} &  -2\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(1,-1,5)\cdot(-2,5,5)\\\\
&=1 \cdot -2-1\cdot 5 + 5\cdot 5\\\\
&=18
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}18 & -13 \\ 15 & 5\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rrr}5 & 5 & 1 \\ 4 & 0 & 3\end{array}\right]$ and  $\text B = \left[\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 \\ -2 & 3\end{array}\right]$



** Let $\text {H = EB}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{B}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{5} & \maroonC{5} & \maroonC{1} \\ 4 & 0 & 3\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{-1} & 5 \\ \maroonC{1} &  2 \\ \maroonC{-2} &  3\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(5,5,1)\cdot(-1,1,-2)\\\\
&=5 \cdot -1+5\cdot 1 + 1\cdot -2\\\\
&=-2
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

### Try it yourself for $\text{H}_{2,1}$

[[☃ graded-group 1]]
###Summary 

After calculating all the remaining entries of $\text{H}$, we get the following answer.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}-2 & 38 \\ -10 & 29\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
$\text E = \left[\begin{array}{rr}3 & 5 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\text A = \left[\begin{array}{rrr}-2 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 & -2\end{array}\right]$

** Let $\text {H = EA}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{A}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{3} & \maroonC{5} \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{-2} & 2 & 3 \\ \maroonC{3} &  5 & -2\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(3,5)\cdot(-2,3)\\\\
&=3 \cdot -2 + 5\cdot 3\\\\
&=9
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 


$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{F}$ and the first column of $\text{E}$.

$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{1} & \maroonC{2} \\ -2 & 3\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{0} & -1 & 5 \\ \maroonC{3} &  2 & 1\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(1,2)\cdot(0,3)\\\\
&=1 \cdot 0 + 2\cdot 3\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{D}$ and the first column of $\text{A}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{4} & \maroonC{-1} \\ 2 & -1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{3} & 1 & 0 \\ \maroonC{2} &  1 & -2\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(4,-1)\cdot(3,2)\\\\

** Let $\text {H = DC}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 


$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{D}$ and the first column of $\text{C}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{2} & \maroonC{0} \\ 4 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{2} & 4 & 4 \\ \maroonC{4} &  2 & 4\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(2,0)\cdot(2,4)\\\\

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{D}$ and the first column of $\text{B}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{1} & \maroonC{0} \\ -1 & 5\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{1} & 0 & 2 \\ \maroonC{4} &  5 & 4\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(1,0)\cdot(1,4)\\\\

** Let $\text {H = EB}$. Find $\text H$. **

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
###The Strategy 

When multiplying matrices, we should find each entry of the resulting product matrix separately.

To find entry $(i,j)$ of the resulting product matrix, we calculate the vector *dot product* of row $i$ of the first matrix and column $j$ of the second matrix. [[☃ explanation 1]] 

###Finding $\text {H}_{1,1}$ 

$\text{H}_{1,1}$ is the dot product of the first row of $\text{E}$ and the first column of $\text{B}$.

>$ \text {H}=\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonC{-1} & \maroonC{3} \\ 0 & 4\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \maroonC{0} & 5 & -2 \\ \maroonC{5} &  2 & 2\end{array}\right]$

Therefore, this is the appropriate calculation of $\text{H}_{1,1}$.

>$\begin{align}\text{H}_{1,1}&=(-1,3)\cdot(0,5)\\\\
&=-1 \cdot 0 + 3\cdot 5\\\\
&=15
\end{align}$

The other entries of $\text{H}$ can be found similarly.
